title: Individual and group performance
paperName: Paper 3 - Specialist Options: Approaches, Issues and Debates
summary: 9990 Organisational — social facilitation, loafing, and team performance factors.
learningObjectives: Explain social facilitation and audience effects.
Analyse social loafing and free-rider problem.
Evaluate Ringelmann effect and team size.
Assess strategies to maximise group performance.

simpleExplanation.title: Better together — or worse?
simpleExplanation.summary: Presence of others can boost simple task performance but cause loafing on collective tasks — team design matters enormously.
steps: Social facilitation — arousal helps simple tasks.
Social loafing — less effort in groups.

flashcards.back: Presence of others increases arousal — improves performance on well-learned/simple tasks, impairs complex or new tasks.
Reduced individual effort when working collectively — 'free-rider' problem when output is pooled.
As group size increases, individual effort decreases — tug-of-war studies showed bigger teams pulled less per person.
Make contributions identifiable, set individual goals, increase task importance, reduce group size.
Co-action = others doing same task; audience = being watched — both can trigger arousal.
Complex tasks benefiting from diverse skills, when loafing is controlled and goals are clear.

sections.content: **Group performance** (4.3.2) — contrast **facilitation vs loafing** with named studies. Team size and **identifiability** are practical levers.

sections.question: Warehouse staff pick orders faster when a supervisor walks the floor (simple, repetitive task). A team of twelve is tasked with 'improving layout' and one member does most of the measuring while others chat. Analyse using social facilitation and social loafing.
sections.solution:
**Social facilitation (Zajonc):** Supervisor presence **increases arousal** on the **well-learned picking task** → faster performance. If pickers were **new** or routes **complex**, the same arousal could **impair** performance (dominant response not yet skilled).

**Social loafing / Ringelmann:** Layout project is **collective** with **pooled output** — individual contribution hard to see, so **motivation drops** (Latane et al.). Team of twelve amplifies **Ringelmann effect**.

**Solutions:** Assign **named sub-tasks**, **individual deadlines**, **smaller sub-groups**, make outcomes **visible** to manager.

**Evaluation:** Facilitation and loafing are not contradictory — **task type and evaluation potential** determine which dominates. **Culture** may moderate loafing (collectivist groups sometimes show less). Link to **goal-setting** (4.1.2) for collective projects.
